back:
What is AI democratization and why does it change how people build AI applications?

---

AI democratization means making AI more widely accessible to everyday users. The best foundation models are published to open-source communities like Hugging Face. Because these models are so generalizable and powerful out of the box, developers can skip the data-gathering and training steps and go straight to building applications. This dramatically lowers the barrier to entry for AI development.

In the context of ML roles, what does a generative AI engineer do and how does their work differ from a data scientist's?

---

An AI engineer is an "AI system builder." They use foundation models to build generative AI systems that transform business processes. Unlike data scientists who focus on extracting insights from data, AI engineers focus on prescriptive use cases (choosing optimal actions) and generative use cases (creating content, assistants, chatbots).

front: In Scikit-learn, a [...] is used to evaluate model accuracy by comparing predicted labels against actual labels for the test set.
back: In Scikit-learn, a **confusion matrix** is used to evaluate model accuracy by comparing predicted labels against actual labels for the test set.

What are the steps in a typical data science process, and why does each step matter?

---

1. Start with a use case. 2. Pick the right data. 3. Prepare the data (cleaning, preprocessing). 4. Train and validate a model using techniques like feature engineering, cross-validation, and hyperparameter tuning. 5. Deploy the model to an endpoint (e.g., in the cloud) for real-time prediction and inference. Each use case requires gathering different data and training a different model.

In the context of ML roles, what does a data scientist do and what kind of output do they produce?

---

A data scientist is a "data storyteller." They take massive amounts of messy real-world data and use mathematical models to translate that data into insights. Their work centers on descriptive analytics (understanding the past) and predictive analytics (forecasting outcomes).

Name four popular deep learning frameworks and what distinguishes them.

---

1. TensorFlow: open-source library for numerical computing and large-scale ML. 2. Keras: easy-to-use library for implementing neural networks. 3. PyTorch: open-source library for deep learning, computer vision, and NLP; supports experimentation. 4. Theano: for efficiently defining, optimizing, and evaluating mathematical expressions involving arrays.

What is the difference between descriptive and predictive use cases in data science?

---

Descriptive use cases describe the past -- for example, exploratory data analysis (EDA) with graphs and statistical inference, or clustering to group similar data points (like customer segmentation). Predictive use cases forecast the future -- for example, regression models that predict a numeric value (temperature, revenue) or classification models that predict a categorical value (success/failure).

front: etl vs elt why elt won
back:
ETL (Extract → Transform → Load) was the standard data pipeline approach for decades. What is ELT, how does it differ, and why has it become the modern default?

---

**ELT** (Extract → Load → Transform) dumps raw data straight into the data warehouse first, then transforms it there using the warehouse's own compute.

ETL transformed data on a separate server *before* loading, which made sense when warehouse compute was expensive and storage was the bottleneck.

ELT won because cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) are now extremely powerful at processing data, storage is cheap, and loading raw data first gives you flexibility — you can re-transform without re-extracting, and you can run multiple different transforms on the same raw data. Tools like dbt make the in-warehouse SQL transformation step straightforward.

Why are foundation models described as having "wide scope" compared to traditional ML models?

---

Foundation models can generalize to a wide range of tasks without needing to be retrained for each one. Traditional ML models have "narrow scope" -- each use case requires a different dataset and a differently trained model, making it harder for them to generalize past the domain of their training data.

How does the generative AI development process differ from the traditional data science process?

---

The gen AI process also starts with a use case, but then skips directly to working with a pre-trained foundation model (no need to gather data and train from scratch). The engineer uses prompt engineering to interact with the model, then applies frameworks like prompt chaining, PEFT, RAG, or autonomous agents to build larger AI systems. The final step is embedding the AI into a system or workflow (assistants, applications, automations).

What is the difference between Matplotlib and Seaborn for data visualization?

---

Matplotlib is an extensive foundational library for generating customizable plots and interactive visualizations -- it gives you low-level control. Seaborn is built on top of Matplotlib and provides a high-level interface for drawing attractive statistical graphics with less code. Think of Matplotlib as the engine and Seaborn as a convenient wrapper focused on statistical plots.

Match these ML programming languages to their primary strengths: Julia, Scala, Java, JavaScript.

---

Julia: high-performance language with parallel and distributed numerical computing, used by researchers. Scala: scalable language widely used for big data processing and building ML pipelines. Java: multi-purpose language supporting scalable ML applications deployed in production. JavaScript: used to run ML models in web browsers for client-side applications.

Machine learning tools can be divided into seven categories. Name them.

---

1. Data processing and analytics. 2. Data visualization. 3. Machine learning (classical). 4. Deep learning. 5. Computer vision. 6. Natural language processing (NLP). 7. Generative AI.

What does NumPy provide that makes it foundational for the Python ML ecosystem?

---

NumPy provides efficient numerical computations on large, multidimensional data arrays. It includes comprehensive mathematical functions, random number generators, and linear algebra routines. It also supports GPU-based computing platforms. SciPy, Pandas, Matplotlib, and Scikit-learn are all built on top of NumPy.

What is the central data structure in Pandas, and what role does Pandas play in a machine learning workflow?

---

The central data structure is the DataFrame, a tabular structure for cleaning and transforming structured data. Pandas is used for exploring, wrangling, visualizing, and preparing data for machine learning. It is built on NumPy and Matplotlib.

front: The technique for adapting a foundation model to domain-specific data without retraining all its parameters is called [...].
back: The technique for adapting a foundation model to domain-specific data without retraining all its parameters is called **parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)**.

front: In Scikit-learn, a trained model can be saved for later reuse by exporting it as a [...] file.
back: In Scikit-learn, a trained model can be saved for later reuse by exporting it as a **pickle** file.

What is the difference between prescriptive and generative use cases in AI engineering?

---

Prescriptive use cases are about choosing the best course of action -- examples include decision optimization (assessing possible actions and picking the optimal one) and recommendation engines. Generative use cases are about creating new content -- examples include intelligent assistants, chatbots, conversational search, and content summarization.

front: In generative AI, the process of interacting with foundation models via natural language instructions to make them perform various tasks is called [...].
back: In generative AI, the process of interacting with foundation models via natural language instructions to make them perform various tasks is called **prompt engineering**.

Why is Python the most widely used programming language for machine learning?

---

Python has an extensive collection of libraries for analyzing and processing data, and it makes developing machine learning models easy. Its ecosystem includes NumPy, Pandas, SciPy, Matplotlib, and Scikit-learn, which together cover the full ML pipeline from data wrangling to model deployment.

front: The technique that grounds a foundation model's answers in truth by retrieving relevant information before generating a response is called [...].
back: The technique that grounds a foundation model's answers in truth by retrieving relevant information before generating a response is called **retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)**.

In predictive ML, what is the difference between regression and classification models?

---

Regression models predict a numeric (continuous) value, such as a temperature or revenue amount. Classification models predict a categorical value, such as success or failure. Both are supervised learning techniques, but they differ in the type of output they produce.

front: Scikit-learn is built on three foundational Python libraries: [...], [...], and [...].
back: Scikit-learn is built on three foundational Python libraries: **NumPy**, **SciPy**, and **Matplotlib**.

front: Scikit-learn offers four main categories of algorithms: [...], [...], [...], and [...].
back: Scikit-learn offers four main categories of algorithms: **classification**, **regression**, **clustering**, and **dimensionality reduction**.

What does Scikit-learn offer and what is it built on?

---

Scikit-learn is a free, open-source Python library for building classical machine learning models. It provides classification, regression, clustering, and dimensionality reduction algorithms. It is built on NumPy, SciPy, and Matplotlib. It covers the full ML pipeline: data preprocessing, train/test splitting, model fitting, hyperparameter tuning with cross-validation, prediction, evaluation, and model export.

What are the steps in a basic Scikit-learn machine learning workflow?

---

1. Preprocess and scale the data (e.g., StandardScaler). 2. Split into train and test sets (e.g., 33% for testing). 3. Instantiate a model (e.g., SVC with parameters like gamma and C). 4. Train the model with .fit() on training data. 5. Generate predictions with .predict() on test data. 6. Evaluate with metrics (e.g., confusion matrix). 7. Save the model as a pickle file for later use.

front: SciPy is built on [...] and provides modules for scientific computing tasks such as optimization, integration, and linear regression.
back: SciPy is built on **NumPy** and provides modules for scientific computing tasks such as optimization, integration, and linear regression.

What is the difference between Apache Spark and Hadoop for big data processing?

---

Hadoop is a disk-based solution for storing and batch-processing massive data. Spark is an in-memory data processing framework for real-time big data processing. Spark supports DataFrames and SQL, and is faster and easier to use than Hadoop because it processes data in memory rather than reading/writing to disk.

Why do data scientists and AI engineers tend to work with different types of data?

---

Data scientists primarily work with structured (tabular) data, typically hundreds to hundreds of thousands of observations, which requires heavy cleaning and preprocessing. AI engineers primarily work with unstructured data (text, images, video, audio) because foundation models like LLMs need billions to trillions of tokens to train. The data type aligns with the models each role uses.

What are the key differences between traditional ML models and foundation models?

---

Traditional ML models: hundreds of algorithms to choose from, narrow scope (hard to generalize past their training domain), smaller in size, less compute to train, train in seconds to hours. Foundation models: essentially one model type, wide scope (generalize across many tasks without retraining), billions of parameters, require hundreds to thousands of GPUs, train in weeks to months.

In supervised learning, why do you split your dataset into training and testing sets?

---

You split the data so you can train the model on one portion and then evaluate the model's accuracy on a separate portion it has never seen. This tests whether the model can generalize to new data rather than just memorizing the training examples. In Scikit-learn, this can be done in one line of code.

front: why gen ai split from data science
back:
Why did AI engineering emerge as a field separate from data science?

---

The breakthroughs in generative AI pushed the boundaries of what AI can do so significantly that it split off into its own distinct field. Foundation models introduced a fundamentally different workflow: instead of gathering data and training narrow models per use case, AI engineers work with powerful pre-trained models and use techniques like prompt engineering, RAG, and PEFT to build systems.

How can you determine what kind of regression model fits your data?

---

Analyse **scatter plots** of your target variable against each input variable to visually identify patterns. Try to express those patterns as mathematical functions (linear, exponential, logarithmic, sinusoidal). Generate models and analyse results. If the data has no clear relationship with the target, no regression model may be appropriate.

In multiple regression, what is collinearity and why does it cause problems for what-if analysis?

---

Collinearity is when two independent variables are correlated — they predict each other. This is a problem because what-if analysis requires changing one variable while holding others constant. If two variables are collinear, you can't change one without the other also changing realistically. The solution is to **remove redundant variables** from the model.

In logistic regression, the sigmoid gives you a probability. How do you turn that into a class prediction?

---

By setting a **decision boundary** — a threshold (e.g. 0.5). If the predicted probability is greater than the threshold, assign class 1; otherwise, assign class 0.

What is gradient descent, and how does it find the best parameters?

---

Gradient descent is an iterative method for finding the minimum of a function. It adjusts parameters in the **direction of steepest descent** (the negative of the gradient). A **learning rate** controls how far each step goes. As it approaches the minimum, the slope diminishes to zero.

A logistic regression model predicts p̂ = 0.05 (very confident the answer is class 0), but the true class is 1. Why does log loss penalise this so much more severely than a prediction of p̂ = 0.5?

---

Log loss uses log(p̂) when the true class is 1. The log of values between 0 and 1 is negative, and gets increasingly large in magnitude as the value approaches zero.

- log(0.5) ≈ −0.69 → moderate penalty
- log(0.05) ≈ −3.0 → huge penalty

This asymmetry is intentional: being confidently wrong is far worse than being uncertain. Log loss forces the model to be *honest* about uncertainty — it can't hedge by always predicting 0.5, but it's also punished severely for overconfidence in the wrong direction.

front:
In scikit-learn, the function to calculate log loss (the cost function for logistic regression) is:
`from sklearn.metrics import [...]`
back:
In scikit-learn, the function to calculate log loss (the cost function for logistic regression) is:
`from sklearn.metrics import **log_loss**`

What is log loss, and what behaviour does it reward and penalise?

---

Log loss is the cost function used to optimise logistic regression. It measures how well predicted probabilities match actual classes.

- **Rewards**: Confident predictions that are **correct** (low log loss)
- **Penalises**: Confident predictions that are **incorrect** (very high log loss)

Despite its name, logistic regression is not used for predicting continuous values. What does it actually do?

---

Logistic regression is a **binary classifier**. It predicts the **probability** of an observation belonging to one of two classes (e.g. yes/no, 0/1). By choosing a threshold probability (e.g. 0.5), it converts that probability into a class prediction.

Describe the steps involved in training a logistic regression model.

---

1. Choose a starting set of parameters (theta) — can be random
2. Predict the probability of class 1 for each observation
3. Measure the error using the **log loss** cost function
4. Update theta to reduce the error
5. Repeat until log loss is satisfactory or maximum iterations reached

front: MAE (Mean Absolute Error) is the [...]. It is the easiest metric to understand because it is simply the [...].
back: MAE (Mean Absolute Error) is the **mean of the absolute value of the errors**. It is the easiest metric to understand because it is simply the **average error**.

front: MSE (Mean Squared Error) is the [...]. It is also the metric the model uses to find the best fit line, which is why it is also called the [...].
back: MSE (Mean Squared Error) is the **mean of the squared errors**. It is also the metric the model uses to find the best fit line, which is why it is also called the **residual sum of squares**.

You're building a multiple regression model. Two of your input features have a correlation of 0.85 — high enough to cause multicollinearity. You need to drop one. How do you decide which?

---

Keep the one that:
1. Is more directly related to the target variable (higher correlation with the output)
2. Is easier to measure or collect in practice
3. Makes more theoretical sense for the model

The rule of thumb threshold is a correlation between input variables above **0.8** (some use 0.7) — this signals problematic multicollinearity.

Note: you're checking correlations *between inputs*, not between inputs and the target — high input-target correlation is what you *want*.

What is the main risk of adding too many variables to a multiple regression model?

---

**Overfitting** — the model essentially memorises the training data, including noise and random variation, making it a poor predictor for unseen observations.

Give three real-world examples of nonlinear relationships and the type of function that models each.

---

- **Exponential**: Compound growth — e.g. how investments grow with compound interest
- **Logarithmic**: Diminishing returns — e.g. productivity gains decrease as hours worked increase
- **Sinusoidal**: Seasonal variation — e.g. monthly rainfall or temperature patterns

OLS regression has clear strengths and weaknesses. What are they?

---

**Pros**: Easy to understand and interpret. Requires no tuning — the solution is just a calculation. Fast, especially for smaller datasets.

**Cons**: A linear model may be too simplistic to capture nonlinear relationships. Outliers can greatly reduce accuracy because they get too much weight in the calculations.

There are two common approaches to finding the parameters in multiple linear regression. What are they, and when would you choose each?

---

- **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**: Uses linear algebra to calculate optimal values directly. Best for smaller datasets.
- **Gradient descent**: An iterative optimisation that starts with random values and adjusts. Better for **large datasets** where OLS becomes impractical.

Why is using a high-degree polynomial dangerous, even though it can pass through every data point?

---

A polynomial of sufficiently high degree will pass through every point, but this amounts to **overfitting**. The model memorises everything — including random noise and large variations — rather than understanding the underlying pattern. The goal is to capture the **trend**, not every fine detail.

Polynomial regression looks nonlinear, but it can actually be solved using linear regression. How?

---

By creating new variables from powers of the original feature — e.g. x_1 = x, x_2 = x^2, x_3 = x^3 — the polynomial equation becomes a **linear combination** of these new variables. You can then use ordinary multiple linear regression to find the best-fit parameters.

What is the key difference between polynomial regression and true nonlinear regression?

---

**Polynomial regression** can be transformed into a linear regression problem (by creating polynomial features). It is a special case of nonlinear regression.

**True nonlinear regression** (exponential, logarithmic, sinusoidal) does **not necessarily reduce to linear regression**.

What does the R2-Score tell you about a regression model, and what are its bounds?

---

R2-Score represents how close the data points are to the fitted regression line. The **higher** the R2-Score, the better the model fits the data. The best possible score is **1.0**, and it **can be negative** (because the model can be arbitrarily worse than a horizontal baseline).

Why is RMSE often preferred over MSE when interpreting model performance?

---

RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) is the square root of MSE. It transforms MSE into the **same units as the variables being compared**, making it easier to interpret. If you're predicting house prices in dollars, RMSE is also in dollars.

When standardising features with StandardScaler, you fit on training data and then transform both train and test sets. Why is fitting the scaler on test data — or on the whole dataset — a form of data leakage?

---

If you fit the scaler on the test set (or the full dataset), the scaler learns statistics (mean, standard deviation) that include information from test examples the model hasn't seen yet. Those statistics then influence how training data is transformed, meaning the model has indirectly "seen" the test set during training.

Correct workflow:
```python
scaler.fit_transform(X_train)   # fit + transform training data
scaler.transform(X_test)        # transform test data using the same scaler
```

The scaler fitted on training data is the one that would be deployed — using it on the test set accurately simulates what would happen in production.

Standard gradient descent calculates the gradient over the entire dataset each iteration. What is the faster alternative, and what are its trade-offs?

---

**Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** uses a **random subset** of training data instead.

- **Faster** and scales well to large datasets
- Can be **less accurate** (approximate gradient)
- **More likely to find global minima** (can skip over local minima)
- Convergence improves by decreasing the learning rate as it gets closer to the minimum, or gradually increasing the sample size

In logistic regression, why can't you just use a straight line to predict class membership? What function solves this?

---

A straight line's predictions grow indefinitely — they aren't bounded between 0 and 1, so they can't represent probabilities.

The **sigmoid function** (sigma(x) = 1 / (1 + e^(-x))) solves this by taking any value and continuously compressing it into the range 0 to 1, producing a valid probability.

front:
In simple linear regression, the model is expressed as an equation of a line.

The predicted value y-hat = [...] + [...] * x_1, where theta_0 is the [...] (y-intercept) and theta_1 is the [...] (weight).
back:
In simple linear regression, the model is expressed as an equation of a line.

The predicted value y-hat = **theta_0** + **theta_1** * x_1, where theta_0 is the **bias coefficient** (y-intercept) and theta_1 is the **slope** (weight).

There are two main categories of regression based on the number of input variables. What are they, and how do they differ?

---

- **Simple regression**: Uses a single independent variable to estimate the dependent variable. Can be linear or nonlinear.
- **Multiple regression**: Uses two or more independent variables. Also can be linear or nonlinear.

The distinction is purely about how many input features the model uses.

front:
In scikit-learn, both simple and multiple linear regression use the same class.

To create and train a linear regression model:
`from sklearn.linear_model import [...]`
back:
In scikit-learn, both simple and multiple linear regression use the same class.

To create and train a linear regression model:
`from sklearn.linear_model import **LinearRegression**`

front:
In scikit-learn, the class for logistic regression is:
`from sklearn.linear_model import [...]`
back:
In scikit-learn, the class for logistic regression is:
`from sklearn.linear_model import **LogisticRegression**`

front:
To do polynomial regression in scikit-learn, you first transform the features, then use linear regression.

The feature transformation uses: `from sklearn.preprocessing import [...]`
back:
To do polynomial regression in scikit-learn, you first transform the features, then use linear regression.

The feature transformation uses: `from sklearn.preprocessing import **PolynomialFeatures**`

front: `StandardScaler` standardises features by removing the [...] and scaling to [...].
back: `StandardScaler` standardises features by removing the **mean** and scaling to **unit variance**.

front: `train_test_split` splits the dataset into [...] and [...] subsets to evaluate the model's performance.
back: `train_test_split` splits the dataset into **training** and **testing** subsets to evaluate the model's performance.

When selecting variables for a multiple regression model, what should you look for?

---

Use a balanced approach: choose variables that are **uncorrelated** with each other, **well understood**, **controllable**, and **most correlated with the target** variable.

Linear regression aims to find the line that minimises the mean of all residual errors. What is this approach called, and what does it minimise?

---

**Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression**. It minimises the Mean Squared Error (MSE) — the average of the squared differences between predicted and actual values.

What is regression in machine learning?

---

Regression is a type of supervised learning that models the relationship between a **continuous target variable** and explanatory features. It's used to predict continuous values — for example, CO2 emissions, sales forecasts, house prices, or the spread of infectious diseases.

In linear regression, the model rarely predicts perfectly. What is the residual error, and what does it tell you?

---

The residual error is the **vertical distance** from a data point to the fitted regression line. It measures how far off the prediction is for that individual point. The average of all residual errors tells you how poorly the line fits the data overall.

When is logistic regression a good choice for your problem?

---

Three situations:
1. When the **target is binary** (0 or 1)
2. When you need the **probability** of an outcome (not just a class label)
3. When you want to **understand the impact** of each feature — logistic regression lets you select important features based on the size of their model coefficients

Why does multiple linear regression generally result in a better model than simple linear regression?

---

Multiple linear regression uses **two or more independent variables**, so it can account for multiple factors influencing the outcome. It also assigns a relative importance (weight) to each feature, revealing how strongly each one affects the target.

What is bagging (bootstrap aggregating), and which problem does it solve?

---

Bagging trains multiple models in parallel on different bootstrapped (randomly sampled with replacement) subsets of the training data, then averages their predictions. It reduces prediction variance and lowers the risk of overfitting. The base learners should have low bias (so they're accurate) but may have high variance (which bagging corrects).

When would you choose bagging over boosting, and vice versa?

---

Choose bagging (e.g., random forests) when your model overfits -- it reduces variance by averaging parallel models trained on bootstrapped data. Choose boosting (e.g., XGBoost, AdaBoost) when your model underfits -- it reduces bias by sequentially training weak learners that correct each other's errors. Bagging trains in parallel; boosting trains sequentially.

front: Using the dartboard analogy: bias corresponds to [...] (how close darts are to the center). Variance corresponds to [...] (how close the darts are to each other). The best model has both low bias and low variance.
back: Using the dartboard analogy: bias corresponds to **accuracy** (how close darts are to the center). Variance corresponds to **precision** (how close the darts are to each other). The best model has both low bias and low variance.

front: In ML, prediction bias measures how far a model's predictions are from the actual target values on average. Think of bias as how [...] or [...] the model is. A perfect predictor has [...] bias.
back: In ML, prediction bias measures how far a model's predictions are from the actual target values on average. Think of bias as how **on-target** or **off-target** the model is. A perfect predictor has **zero** bias.

Explain the bias-variance tradeoff as model complexity increases.

---

As model complexity increases, bias decreases (the model fits training data better) while variance increases (the model becomes more sensitive to particular training data). Low complexity causes underfitting (high bias). High complexity causes overfitting (high variance). There is a crossover point where total error is minimized -- some irreducible error (e.g., random noise) will always remain.

What is the difference between a binary classifier and a multi-class classifier?

---

A binary classifier predicts one of two possible classes (e.g., will default or will not default). A multi-class classifier predicts one of three or more classes (e.g., which of three drugs to prescribe). Many algorithms are natively binary and require strategies like one-vs-all or one-vs-one to handle multiple classes.

What is boosting, and which problem does it solve?

---

Boosting is an ensemble method that builds a series of weak learners sequentially, where each new learner focuses on correcting the errors of the previous one. It reduces prediction bias (helps with underfitting). After each iteration, misclassified data points get higher weights so the next learner focuses on them. The final model is a weighted sum of all weak learners.

What is classification in machine learning, and what kind of target variable does it predict?

---

Classification is a supervised ML method that uses trained models to predict labels on new data. The target variable is categorical with discrete values (e.g., "default" / "no default", or drug A / drug B / drug C).

What are the key differences between a classification tree and a regression tree?

---

Classification tree: target is categorical; leaf prediction is a majority vote among class labels; split quality is measured by entropy/information gain or Gini impurity. Regression tree: target is continuous; leaf prediction is the average of target values in the node; split quality is measured by mean squared error (MSE), which captures variance within each node.

front: The distinguishing feature between classification and regression is the target variable. In classification, the target is [...] (e.g., true/false, drug A/B). In regression, the target is [...] (e.g., temperature, salary).
back: The distinguishing feature between classification and regression is the target variable. In classification, the target is **categorical** (e.g., true/false, drug A/B). In regression, the target is **continuous** (e.g., temperature, salary).

Why are decision trees considered highly interpretable compared to other classifiers?

---

Decision trees can be visualized as flowcharts, so you can see exactly how each decision is made. Since the tree grows by selecting the next best feature to split on, you can also see how important or predictive each feature is.

Why would you prune a decision tree?

---

An overly complex tree overfits the training data by capturing noise and irrelevant details. Pruning simplifies the tree, improves generalization to unseen data, makes it more concise and interpretable, and results in better predictive accuracy.

front: The two common splitting criteria for decision trees are [...] and [...].
back: The two common splitting criteria for decision trees are **information gain (entropy reduction)** and **Gini impurity**.

front: Pre-emptive tree pruning stops a decision tree from growing when a stopping criterion is met. Four common stopping criteria are: maximum [...] is reached, minimum number of [...] is exceeded, minimum number of [...] is exceeded, and maximum number of [...] is reached.
back: Pre-emptive tree pruning stops a decision tree from growing when a stopping criterion is met. Four common stopping criteria are: maximum **tree depth** is reached, minimum number of **data points in a node** is exceeded, minimum number of **samples in a leaf** is exceeded, and maximum number of **leaf nodes** is reached.

In a decision tree, what do internal nodes, branches, and leaf nodes each represent?

---

Each internal node corresponds to a test on a feature. Each branch corresponds to the result of that test. Each leaf (terminal) node assigns the data point to a class. The tree works like a flowchart, routing data from root to leaf through a series of feature-based decisions.

How is a decision tree trained (grown)?

---

Start with a root node and all labeled training data. At each node, find the feature that best splits the data into its labeled classes according to a splitting criterion. Each split partitions the data, and each partition is passed to a new child node. Repeat for each new node (using each feature only once) until all nodes are pure, you run out of features, or a stopping criterion is met.

Why are decision trees commonly chosen as base learners in ensemble methods like bagging and boosting?

---

Decision trees' bias and variance can be easily adjusted by changing their depth. Shallow trees provide low bias (good for bagging) or high bias with low variance (good as weak learners in boosting). This tunability makes them flexible building blocks for both random forests and gradient boosting.

What does entropy measure in the context of decision trees, and what do its extreme values (0 and 1) mean?

---

Entropy measures the disorder or randomness of the classes in a node -- how uncertain the split result is. An entropy of 0 means the node is completely pure (all data points belong to one class). An entropy of 1 means the classes are equally divided (maximum uncertainty).

front: Information gain and entropy are [...] in direction. As entropy decreases, information gain [...].
back: Information gain and entropy are **opposites** in direction. As entropy decreases, information gain **increases**.

Your dataset has a severe class imbalance (e.g. 95% negative, 5% positive). What are four distinct strategies for handling this, and what does each one do?

---

1. **Resampling** — oversample the minority class (e.g. SMOTE generates synthetic minority examples) or undersample the majority class to create a more balanced training set.

2. **Better evaluation metrics** — switch from accuracy to precision, recall, F1, or AUC-ROC, which are sensitive to performance on the minority class.

3. **Cost-sensitive learning** — adjust the learning algorithm to penalise misclassifying the minority class more heavily (e.g. `class_weight='balanced'` in sklearn).

4. **Ensemble methods** — bagging and boosting can improve minority class performance by combining multiple models.

You're building a fraud detection model where only 1% of transactions are fraudulent. A model that predicts "not fraud" for every single transaction achieves 99% accuracy. What's wrong with using accuracy here, and what should you use instead?

---

Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced datasets because it rewards a model for just predicting the majority class every time. A 99% accurate fraud detector that never catches any fraud is useless.

Better alternatives:
- **Precision** — of all predicted fraud cases, how many were real?
- **Recall** — of all actual fraud cases, how many did we catch?
- **F1 score** — balances both precision and recall
- **AUC-ROC** — measures discrimination ability across all decision thresholds

What is information gain in a decision tree, and why does the algorithm maximize it?

---

Information gain is the entropy before a split minus the weighted entropy after the split. It measures how much certainty a feature adds. The algorithm selects the feature with the highest information gain at each node because that feature best separates the data into its classes, producing the purest child nodes.

Why is feature scaling critical for KNN?

---

KNN relies on distance calculations between data points. Features with large numerical ranges will dominate the distance measure and disproportionately influence predictions, making them artificially more important. Standardization scales features so they contribute equally to the distance metric.

How do you find the optimal value of K for a KNN model?

---

Test a range of K values using a labeled test dataset. For each K, train the model on the training set and measure prediction accuracy on the test set. Choose the K that gives the best accuracy. You can also check whether adding or removing a feature changes model performance for a given K.

How does KNN make a classification prediction for a new data point?

---

1. Pick a value for K. 2. Calculate the distance from the new point to all labeled points in the training data. 3. Find the K nearest neighbors. 4. Predict the class by majority vote among those K neighbors. For regression, predict using the average or median of the neighbors' target values instead.

Why does including irrelevant features hurt KNN more than many other algorithms?

---

Irrelevant features act like noise in the distance calculation, distorting which points appear "nearest." This requires a higher K to compensate, which increases computational cost and diminishes accuracy. Keeping only relevant features (using domain knowledge) lowers the optimal K and improves both accuracy and efficiency.

Why does a small K value cause overfitting and a large K value cause underfitting in KNN?

---

A small K makes predictions highly sensitive to individual nearby points, capturing noise and local fluctuations (overfitting / high variance). A large K smooths out finer details by averaging over many neighbors, potentially washing out real patterns (underfitting / high bias). The optimal K lies somewhere in between, found by testing a range of values on a labeled test set.

Why is KNN called a "lazy learner," and how does that differ from eager learners like decision trees?

---

KNN doesn't build an internal model during training -- it simply stores all the training data. At prediction time, it computes distances to every training point, sorts them, and selects the top K. Eager learners like decision trees and SVMs train an explicit model first, then use it to predict without revisiting the raw training data.

Why does KNN's majority voting fail with skewed class distributions, and how can you fix it?

---

When one class is much more frequent, its data points dominate the K nearest neighbors simply because there are more of them nearby. This causes predictions to be biased toward the majority class. A fix is to weigh each neighbor's vote by its distance to the query point, so closer neighbors have more influence regardless of class frequency.

How does the one-versus-all (one-vs-rest) strategy extend a binary classifier to handle multiple classes?

---

It trains one binary classifier per class. Each classifier decides whether a data point belongs to its assigned class or not. For k classes, there are exactly k binary classifiers. A data point that no classifier claims can be treated as an outlier or noise.

front: one vs all vs one vs one
back:
When would you choose one-vs-all over one-vs-one for multi-class classification?

---

One-vs-all is simpler and more scalable because it trains only k classifiers (one per class), while one-vs-one trains k(k-1)/2 classifiers (one per pair), which becomes expensive as the number of classes grows. However, one-vs-one can work well for small datasets. One-vs-all may also be preferred when one-vs-one produces ties.

How does the one-versus-one strategy extend a binary classifier to handle multiple classes?

---

It trains a binary classifier for every pair of classes. Each classifier decides between two classes using only the data from those two classes. The final label is decided by a voting scheme -- typically the class predicted most often wins. Ties can be broken by weighting votes by confidence.

front: Three popular boosting algorithms are [...], [...], and [...].
back: Three popular boosting algorithms are **Gradient Boosting**, **XGBoost**, and **AdaBoost**.

How does a random forest work, and why does it use shallow trees?

---

A random forest is a bagging method that trains multiple decision trees on bootstrapped datasets. The trees don't need to be deep. Shallow trees have low bias but high variance; aggregating many of them significantly reduces variance while only slightly increasing bias. This makes the ensemble more stable and resistant to overfitting than any single deep tree.

front: In a regression tree, the predicted value at a leaf node is the [...] of the actual target values of the data points in that node. For skewed data, the [...] may be preferable but is more expensive to compute.
back: In a regression tree, the predicted value at a leaf node is the **average** of the actual target values of the data points in that node. For skewed data, the **median** may be preferable but is more expensive to compute.

Why does a regression tree use mean squared error (MSE) instead of entropy to measure split quality?

---

Entropy measures disorder among categorical classes, which doesn't apply to continuous targets. MSE measures the variance of target values within each node -- how spread out the actual values are from the predicted (average) value. Lower MSE means the values in a node are more tightly grouped, indicating a higher-quality split.

How can you choose candidate thresholds for splitting a continuous feature in a regression tree?

---

Sort the feature values and remove duplicates. Define candidate thresholds as the midpoints between each pair of consecutive values. For each candidate, calculate the weighted MSE of the resulting split and choose the threshold that minimizes it. This is an exhaustive search that doesn't scale well to big data, so for very large datasets you can sample a sparse subset of thresholds.

How is the quality of a regression tree split measured using weighted MSE?

---

Weighted MSE = (1 / total observations) * [n_left * MSE_left + n_right * MSE_right], where n_left and n_right are the number of observations in each child node. The split with the lowest weighted MSE is chosen because it minimizes the variance in predicted values across both child nodes.

What are the main advantages and limitations of SVM?

---

Advantages: effective in high-dimensional spaces, robust to overfitting, excels on linearly separable data, and works with weakly separable data using the soft margin option. Limitations: slow for training on large datasets, sensitive to noise and overlapping classes, and sensitive to the choice of kernel and regularization parameters, which are non-trivial to determine.

Once an SVM is trained and produces a weight vector w and bias term b, how do you classify a new point x?

---

Plug x into the equation: w^T * x + b. If the result is greater than 0, the point belongs to the class above the hyperplane. If less than 0, it belongs to the class below. The sign of the output determines the class assignment.

What is the core idea behind a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier?

---

SVM finds the hyperplane that separates two classes while maximizing the margin -- the distance from the hyperplane to the closest data points from each class. These closest points are called support vectors. In a 2D feature space, the hyperplane is a line; in higher dimensions, it is a plane or hyperplane.

front: Scikit-learn provides four kernel functions for SVM: [...] (default, standard SVM), [...] (for parabolic embedding), [...] (radial basis function, scores high for nearby points with exponential decay), and [...] (same function used in logistic regression).
back: Scikit-learn provides four kernel functions for SVM: **linear** (default, standard SVM), **polynomial** (for parabolic embedding), **RBF** (radial basis function, scores high for nearby points with exponential decay), and **sigmoid** (same function used in logistic regression).

Why would you use kerneling in SVM, and what does it do?

---

When data is not linearly separable in its original feature space, kerneling maps the data into a higher-dimensional space where a linear separating hyperplane can be found. For example, two concentric circular classes in 2D can be separated by a horizontal plane after being mapped to 3D using a polynomial kernel.

In SVM, what does the C parameter control, and what happens at its extremes?

---

C controls the balance between maximizing the margin and minimizing misclassifications (the soft margin tradeoff). A smaller C allows more misclassifications, creating a softer margin and more tolerance for noisy data. A larger C enforces stricter separation, creating a harder margin but risking overfitting to noise.

front: In an SVM, the [...] are the nearest data points from each class to the decision boundary. The [...] is defined as the distance from the hyperplane to these points.
back: In an SVM, the **support vectors** are the nearest data points from each class to the decision boundary. The **margin** is defined as the distance from the hyperplane to these points.
SVM is well-suited for **image classification**, **handwritten digit recognition**, **spam detection**, and **sentiment analysis**.

In Support Vector Regression (SVR), what is the epsilon tube and what role does it play?

---

The epsilon tube is a margin around the SVR prediction curve. Points falling inside the tube are treated as signal (acceptable predictions), while points falling outside are interpreted as noise. A larger epsilon creates a wider tube that is more tolerant of deviations; a smaller epsilon demands closer fits.

Using the bias-variance framework, what causes underfitting versus overfitting?

---

Underfitting happens when model complexity is too low, causing high bias -- the model cannot capture the patterns in the training data and makes poor predictions even on training data. Overfitting happens when model complexity is too high, causing high variance -- the model becomes overly sensitive to the training data, capturing noise, and performs poorly on unseen data.

What does prediction variance measure in machine learning?

---

Prediction variance measures how much a model's predictions fluctuate when trained on different subsets of the same dataset. High variance means the model is extremely sensitive to which training data it sees, causing it to overfit and track noise. Low variance means predictions are stable and the model generalizes well to unseen data.

What is the difference between a weak learner and a strong learner?

---

A weak learner performs only slightly better than random guessing. It has high bias and low variance, leading to underfitting. A strong learner has low bias and high variance, meaning it fits training data well but tends to overfit. Ensemble methods combine multiple weak learners to create strong learners.

Walk through the steps of the agglomerative hierarchical clustering algorithm.

---

1. Select a distance metric (e.g., distance between centroids).
2. Initialize N clusters, each containing a single data point.
3. Compute an N-by-N distance matrix showing distances between all pairs.
4. Merge the two closest clusters based on the chosen metric.
5. Update the distance matrix with new distances.
6. Repeat steps 4-5 until the desired number of clusters is reached or all points are in one cluster.

The merge history is visualized as a dendrogram.

What is the difference between agglomerative and divisive hierarchical clustering?

---

**Agglomerative** (bottom-up): Each data point starts as its own cluster. The two closest clusters are merged repeatedly until the desired number of clusters is reached or everything is one cluster.

**Divisive** (top-down): All data starts in one cluster. It is repeatedly split into smaller clusters until a stopping criterion (e.g., minimum cluster size) is met.

Both produce a dendrogram showing the hierarchy of merges or splits.

front: The K-Means objective function measures [...]. Three heuristic techniques for choosing the best K are [...], the [...], and the [...].
back: The K-Means objective function measures **within-cluster variance**. Three heuristic techniques for choosing the best K are **silhouette analysis**, the **elbow method**, and the **Davies-Bouldin index**.

Name five practical applications of clustering.

---

1. **Customer segmentation** for targeted marketing
2. **Anomaly detection** (fraud, equipment malfunctions)
3. **Image compression** by replacing data points with cluster centers
4. **Feature engineering** -- creating new features or reducing dimensionality
5. **Data summarization** -- simplifying data into representative clusters

How can clustering be used for feature selection?

---

By clustering features (not data points), you can identify groups of similar or correlated features that provide redundant information. You then select one representative feature from each cluster, reducing the total number of features while preserving the information each group carries. This is a form of dimension reduction that uses existing features rather than creating new transformed ones (as PCA does).

Both clustering and classification group data into categories. What is the key difference between them?

---

Classification is supervised -- it learns from labeled data to predict categories. Clustering is unsupervised -- it finds natural groupings in unlabeled data by detecting patterns on its own, without knowing the "right" answer.

How do clustering, dimension reduction, and feature engineering complement each other?

---

- **Clustering** helps with feature selection (grouping redundant features) and creation (cluster labels as new features), supporting dimension reduction.
- **Dimension reduction** simplifies high-dimensional data as a preprocessing step for clustering, and helps visualize clustering results.
- **Feature engineering** benefits from both: clustering identifies redundant features to remove, and dimension reduction creates compact new features.

Together they improve model performance, quality, and interpretability.

How does the curse of dimensionality specifically affect distance-based clustering algorithms?

---

As the number of dimensions increases, volume expands rapidly, causing data points to become sparse. When points are sparse, the distances between them become more uniform -- points are neither clearly "close" nor clearly "far" from each other. This makes it hard for distance-based algorithms like K-Means and DBSCAN to form meaningful clusters, because the distance metric loses its discriminating power. The result is smaller, less coherent clusters that need more data to fill the gaps.

What is DBSCAN and what problem does it solve that K-Means cannot?

---

DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) finds clusters by identifying connected regions of relatively high density. Unlike K-Means, it can discover clusters of any shape, does not require specifying the number of clusters, and can label isolated points as noise rather than forcing them into a cluster.

How does DBSCAN's cluster-building process differ from K-Means in terms of iteration?

---

DBSCAN is not iterative. It grows clusters in a single pass by expanding from core points to their neighbors, and once a point is labeled it is never updated. K-Means, by contrast, iteratively reassigns points and updates centroids until convergence.

front: DBSCAN requires two user-specified parameters: [...], which is the radius of each neighborhood, and [...], the minimum number of points required in a neighborhood to form a cluster.
back: DBSCAN requires two user-specified parameters: **epsilon (eps)**, which is the radius of each neighborhood, and **min_samples (n)**, the minimum number of points required in a neighborhood to form a cluster.

DBSCAN classifies every data point as one of three types. Explain each.

---

1. **Core point**: Has at least n points (including itself) within its epsilon-radius neighborhood. It is a focal point within a cluster.
2. **Border point**: Falls within the neighborhood of a core point but does not have enough neighbors to be a core point itself. It sits on the outer edge of a cluster.
3. **Noise point**: Is isolated from all core point neighborhoods. It is not assigned to any cluster.

DBSCAN uses a fixed epsilon radius everywhere. Why is this a problem when clusters have varying densities?

---

A single epsilon value that works for a high-density region will be too small for a low-density region (missing points), and one that works for a low-density region will be too large for a high-density region (merging distinct clusters). In the Canadian museums example, DBSCAN lumped a large, dense eastern region into a single cluster because the fixed epsilon could not adapt to the higher local density. HDBSCAN solves this by adjusting the neighborhood size to local density.

What is a dendrogram and what does it reveal about hierarchical clustering?

---

A dendrogram is a tree-like diagram that visualizes the hierarchy of cluster merges (agglomerative) or splits (divisive). It reveals the relationships between clusters -- which groups were combined or separated at each step, and at what distance. This lets you see the nested structure of clusters and choose how many clusters to keep by cutting the tree at a particular level.

Why are dimension reduction techniques needed to evaluate clustering results in high-dimensional data?

---

Clustering results cannot be visualized directly when the feature space has more than three dimensions. Dimension reduction methods like PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP can project high-dimensional clustering outcomes into 2D or 3D scatter plots, making it possible to visually assess cluster quality, identify overlaps, and spot patterns that are hidden in the original high-dimensional space.

Why is dimension reduction used as a preprocessing step before clustering?

---

As dimensionality increases, the volume of the feature space expands rapidly, making data points sparse and less similar to each other (the curse of dimensionality). This is especially problematic for distance-based clustering algorithms like K-Means and DBSCAN. Dimension reduction techniques (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP) reduce the number of features while preserving critical information, making clustering more efficient and effective.

front: In HDBSCAN, cluster stability refers to a cluster's ability to [...].
back: In HDBSCAN, cluster stability refers to a cluster's ability to **persist without changing much when the neighborhood size is adjusted within a reasonable range of radii**.

Describe the high-level steps HDBSCAN uses to find clusters.

---

1. Start by treating each point as its own cluster (effectively all noise).
2. Progressively agglomerate clusters into a hierarchy by incrementally lowering the density threshold.
3. Construct a hierarchical tree from these merges.
4. Simplify into a condensed tree, keeping only the most stable clusters across different density levels.

It is a combination of agglomerative and density-based clustering.

What advantage does HDBSCAN have over DBSCAN, and how does it achieve it?

---

HDBSCAN adapts the neighborhood size to reflect changes in local density, whereas DBSCAN uses a fixed epsilon radius for all regions. HDBSCAN achieves this through **cluster stability** -- it measures how persistent a cluster is over a range of density thresholds. It builds a hierarchy by progressively lowering the density threshold, then keeps only the most stable clusters. This makes it better at handling datasets where density varies across regions.

Walk through the steps of the K-Means algorithm from initialization to convergence.

---

1. **Initialize**: Choose K and randomly select K starting centroid locations.
2. **Assign**: Compute the distance from each data point to each centroid; assign each point to the nearest centroid's cluster.
3. **Update**: Recalculate each centroid as the mean of its cluster's data points.
4. **Repeat**: Iterate steps 2-3 until centroids stop moving (convergence) or a maximum number of iterations is reached.

What assumptions does K-Means make about the data, and what problems arise when those assumptions are violated?

---

K-Means assumes that clusters are **convex** (any line between two points stays within the cluster) and contain **approximately equal numbers of points**. When clusters are imbalanced, the smaller cluster's centroid drifts toward the larger cluster. When clusters are non-convex, K-Means cannot separate them correctly. K-Means is also sensitive to outliers because variance is affected by extreme values.

front: In clustering, a set of points is convex if [...]. K-Means assumes all clusters have this property.
back: In clustering, a set of points is convex if **any line drawn between two points in the set remains entirely within the set**. K-Means assumes all clusters have this property.

What is K-Means clustering and what does it optimize for?

---

K-Means is an iterative, centroid-based algorithm that partitions data into K non-overlapping clusters. It optimizes by minimizing the within-cluster variance for all clusters simultaneously -- each point is assigned to the cluster whose centroid is nearest, and centroids are updated as the mean of their assigned points until positions stabilize.

Why does K-Means struggle with imbalanced clusters (e.g., one cluster has 200 points and another has 10)?

---

The centroid of the smaller cluster drifts toward the larger cluster over iterations. Because K-Means updates centroids as the mean of assigned points, the larger cluster's mass pulls the smaller cluster's centroid closer to it, causing the smaller cluster to gradually consume points from the larger one. The result is a poor partition that does not reflect the true grouping.

front: kmeans vs dbscan when to use
back:
When would you choose DBSCAN over K-Means for a clustering task?

---

Choose DBSCAN when:
- Clusters have **arbitrary (non-convex) shapes** that K-Means cannot separate
- You need to **identify noise/outliers** as separate from clusters
- You **don't know the number of clusters** in advance

Stick with K-Means when clusters are roughly spherical, balanced in size, and you can estimate K, since K-Means is more efficient and scales well to large datasets.

Why can assigning every point to a cluster be a problem, and which clustering family avoids this?

---

Partition-based algorithms (like K-Means) assign every point to a cluster, even outliers that don't truly belong. This forces noise into clusters and can distort cluster shapes and centroids. Density-based algorithms (like DBSCAN) avoid this by labeling isolated points as noise instead of forcing them into the nearest cluster.

When applied to interlocking half-moon shapes, why does partition-based clustering fail while density-based clustering succeeds?

---

Partition-based clustering (like K-Means) assumes clusters are convex -- it draws straight boundaries and cannot separate interlocking curved shapes. Density-based clustering identifies connected regions of high density, so it can follow the curved shape of each half-moon and separate them correctly.

What is the main limitation of PCA, and when does it matter?

---

PCA is a **linear** algorithm -- it assumes features are linearly correlated. When the data contains nonlinear relationships, PCA may lose important information because it cannot capture curved or complex structures in the data. In such cases, nonlinear methods like t-SNE or UMAP are more appropriate.

What is PCA and what kind of data is it best suited for?

---

PCA (Principal Component Analysis) is a linear dimensionality reduction algorithm that transforms features into a new set of uncorrelated variables called principal components, ordered by how much variance they explain. It is best suited for data where features are **linearly correlated**. PCA simplifies data, reduces noise, and minimizes information loss -- the first few components typically capture most of the information while the rest represent noise.

Describe how PCA is applied in the eigenfaces approach to face recognition.

---

PCA is performed on an unlabeled face dataset to extract the top eigenfaces (e.g., 150 out of 966 faces). These eigenfaces form an orthonormal basis for the feature space. The face images are then projected onto this eigenface basis, dramatically reducing dimensionality. A supervised model (such as an SVM) is trained on these reduced features to predict which face is shown. The key benefit is preserving the features needed for identification while minimizing computational load.

Compare PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP in terms of linearity, what structure they preserve, and scalability.

---

| | PCA | t-SNE | UMAP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | Linear | Nonlinear | Nonlinear |
| **Structure preserved** | Global variance | Local (nearby points) | Both local and global |
| **Scalability** | Scales well | Does not scale well | Scales better than t-SNE |
| **Best for** | Linearly correlated features | Visualizing clusters in complex data | General-purpose alternative to t-SNE with better global structure |

PCA works best when features are linearly correlated. For nonlinear data, UMAP generally outperforms t-SNE because it preserves global structure and scales better.

front: PCA produces principal components that are [...] to each other and [...]. They are ordered in [...] order of the variance they explain.
back: PCA produces principal components that are **orthogonal** to each other and **uncorrelated**. They are ordered in **decreasing** order of the variance they explain.

In the context of evaluating K-Means, what does silhouette analysis measure?

---

Silhouette analysis measures how similar a data point is to its own cluster (cohesion) compared to other clusters (separation). It helps gauge whether points are well-matched to their assigned cluster or would fit better in a neighboring one.

What are the three main families of clustering algorithms, and what shape of clusters does each handle best?

---

1. **Partition-based** (e.g., K-Means): produces convex, non-overlapping groups; efficient and scales well to large datasets.
2. **Density-based** (e.g., DBSCAN): can create clusters of any shape; suitable for irregular clusters and noisy data.
3. **Hierarchical** (agglomerative/divisive): organizes data into a tree of nested clusters shown as a dendrogram; intuitive for small to mid-sized datasets.

What is t-SNE and what is it primarily used for?

---

t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) is a nonlinear dimensionality reduction algorithm that maps high-dimensional data to 2D or 3D for visualization. It focuses on preserving the similarity of nearby points (local structure) more than distant points. It works well with complex data like images and text but does not scale well and is sensitive to its hyperparameters (perplexity, learning rate).

front: The key hyperparameters of t-SNE are [...] (balances attention between local and global structure, default 30), [...] (controls step size during optimization, default 200), and [...] (output dimensions, default 2).
back: The key hyperparameters of t-SNE are **perplexity** (balances attention between local and global structure, default 30), **learning_rate** (controls step size during optimization, default 200), and **n_components** (output dimensions, default 2).

t-SNE is often described as a "visualisation technique" rather than a dimensionality reduction method. Why can't you use t-SNE embeddings as input features for a downstream machine learning model?

---

Three reasons make t-SNE output unsuitable for downstream processing:

1. **Stochastic** — results are different every run, so there's no stable representation
2. **Distances not interpretable** — only local neighbourhoods are preserved; between-cluster distances are meaningless numbers
3. **Not reversible / generalisable** — t-SNE fits to the training set; you can't project new points into the same space

Use t-SNE for exploratory scatter plots to identify cluster patterns visually. Use UMAP if you need a stable, generalisable reduction for preprocessing.

Why might UMAP provide better clustering performance than t-SNE?

---

t-SNE focuses on preserving local structure (similarity of nearby points) and can create very distinct-looking clusters even when the original data has overlap. UMAP preserves both local and global structure, so overlapping clusters in the original data appear as overlapping in the reduced space too. This gives a more faithful representation of the true data relationships and often results in higher clustering performance.

What is UMAP and what theoretical idea does it build on?

---

UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) is a nonlinear dimensionality reduction algorithm based on **manifold theory** -- the assumption that high-dimensional data lies on a lower-dimensional manifold embedded in the higher-dimensional space. It constructs a graph of the data in high dimensions, then optimizes a low-dimensional layout that preserves the relationships between points. UMAP preserves both local and global structure and scales better than t-SNE.

front: The key hyperparameters of UMAP are [...] (controls local neighborhood size, default 15), [...] (minimum distance between points in the embedded space, default 0.1), and [...] (dimensionality of the embedding, default 2).
back: The key hyperparameters of UMAP are **n_neighbors** (controls local neighborhood size, default 15), **min_dist** (minimum distance between points in the embedded space, default 0.1), and **n_components** (dimensionality of the embedding, default 2).

Both t-SNE and UMAP are dimensionality reduction techniques used to visualise high-dimensional data. What is the key structural difference between them that makes UMAP the better choice before clustering?

---

**t-SNE** preserves *local* structure well — nearby points stay nearby — but distorts global structure. Distances between clusters in a t-SNE plot are not reliably meaningful.

**UMAP** preserves *both* local and global structure — similar points cluster together AND the relative positions of clusters are meaningful. It's also faster and scales better to large datasets.

For clustering workflows (e.g. before K-means or DBSCAN), UMAP is preferred because the global structure in the reduced space reflects genuine relationships between groups, not just local neighbourhoods.

front: In classification, accuracy is the ratio of [...] to the [...] in the dataset.
back: In classification, accuracy is the ratio of **correctly predicted instances** to the **total number of instances** in the dataset.

front: The adjusted Rand index measures similarity between true labels and clustering outcomes. It ranges from [...]: a score of [...] indicates perfect alignment, [...] indicates random clustering, and negative values indicate worse-than-random performance.
back: The adjusted Rand index measures similarity between true labels and clustering outcomes. It ranges from **-1 to 1**: a score of **1** indicates perfect alignment, **0** indicates random clustering, and negative values indicate worse-than-random performance.

What are the benefits of varying the validation set across folds (as in k-fold CV) rather than using a single fixed validation set?

---

1. Greatly increases the data on which the model trains and tests.
2. Reduces overfitting because it smooths out unwanted details particular to a single chosen training subset.
3. Improves the ability to evaluate how well the model will generalize to unseen data.
4. Model performance is more stable, not dependent on one particular split.

What are four common modeling pitfalls beyond data leakage?

---

1. Using raw data without appropriate feature selection or transformation, preventing discovery of the optimal model.
2. Choosing the wrong evaluation metric or misinterpreting metrics, which misleads evaluation.
3. Failing to address class imbalances in classification, biasing predictions towards the majority class.
4. Blindly relying on automated ML tools without understanding the data and the model the system creates.

What are the four components of a confusion matrix, and what does each represent?

---

- **True Positive (TP)**: predicted positive, actually positive.
- **True Negative (TN)**: predicted negative, actually negative.
- **False Positive (FP)**: predicted positive, actually negative.
- **False Negative (FN)**: predicted negative, actually positive.

The y-axis shows true labels and the x-axis shows predicted labels.

What happens to feature importance rankings when you have highly correlated or redundant features?

---

Correlated or redundant features share importance, which lowers the apparent influence of each individual feature. This can cause you to incorrectly conclude that a significant feature is unimportant. Blindly selecting what seems to be the most important features for subsequent modeling can degrade results.

What is data leakage, and why is it dangerous?

---

Data leakage occurs when a model's training data includes information that would not be available in the real world after deployment. It causes the model to perform misleadingly well during training and validation. Since the test data also contains leaked information, evaluation won't detect the poor generalizability until the model is deployed to production.

What are four strategies to mitigate data leakage?

---

1. Avoid features derived from the entire dataset (like global averages or statistics).
2. Ensure proper separation between training, validation, and test sets with no overlap or contamination.
3. Ensure all features used in the model would be available when making real-world predictions after deployment.
4. When using cross-validation, fit the pipeline separately to each training fold and apply it to the corresponding validation fold (not to the whole dataset at once).

What is data snooping, and why is it harmful?

---

Data snooping is checking your model's performance on the test data before you are done optimizing it. This effectively fits the model to the test data, causing overfitting. The model appears to perform well on the test set but won't generalize to truly unseen data. Data snooping is a form of data leakage.

front: The Davies-Bouldin index measures the average ratio of a cluster's [...] to its [...]. Unlike the silhouette score, [...] values indicate better clustering.
back: The Davies-Bouldin index measures the average ratio of a cluster's **compactness** to its **separation from the nearest cluster**. Unlike the silhouette score, **lower** values indicate better clustering.

Three statements about clustering evaluation metrics — which one is correct?

A) A Davies-Bouldin score of 0.9 is better than a score of 0.3, because higher values indicate better separation.
B) A Silhouette score of −0.4 suggests a point is likely assigned to the wrong cluster.
C) Both Davies-Bouldin and Silhouette scores range from 0 to 1, where 1 is best.

---

**B** — Silhouette scores range from −1 to +1. Negative values mean a point is closer to a neighbouring cluster than its own, suggesting misclassification.

A is wrong: Davies-Bouldin is lower-is-better (0 = perfect, no upper bound). C is wrong: DB has no upper bound, and Silhouette ranges from −1 to +1, not 0 to 1.

front: db vs silhouette level of analysis
back:
Both Davies-Bouldin Index and Silhouette Score measure clustering quality, but they operate at different levels of analysis. What is that distinction, and when does it matter?

---

- **Davies-Bouldin** works at the **cluster level** — it uses centroids to compare clusters as units, averaging the worst-case ratio of within-cluster scatter to between-cluster distance. Good for a quick cluster-level overview.

- **Silhouette** works at the **point level** — it scores each individual data point based on how similar it is to its own cluster vs the nearest other cluster. Can identify specific poorly-assigned points (negative scores = likely misclassified).

When they disagree — e.g. DB looks fine but many Silhouette scores are negative — it means some individual points are badly placed even if the overall cluster structure appears reasonable. Use both for the full picture.

front: In regression, explained variance is the sum of [...] between the [...] and the [...].
back: In regression, explained variance is the sum of **squared differences** between the **predictions** and the **mean of the actual target data**.

F1 score uses the *harmonic* mean of precision and recall, not the arithmetic mean. Why does this matter — what would go wrong with a simple average?

---

The harmonic mean is always closer to the *lower* of the two values. This means you can't get a high F1 by excelling at one metric while being terrible at the other.

Example with arithmetic vs harmonic mean:
- Precision = 0.9, Recall = 0.1
  - Arithmetic mean = 0.50 (misleadingly okay-looking)
  - F1 (harmonic) = 0.18 (correctly terrible)

A model that catches very few positives but is precise when it does, or one that catches everything but makes tons of false alarms, both get punished by F1. You need both precision and recall to be reasonable.

front: The F1 score is the [...] of precision and recall. It is calculated as [...].
back: The F1 score is the **harmonic mean** of precision and recall. It is calculated as **2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)**.

Why is it dangerous to interpret feature importance from a trained model as causation?

---

Feature importance only indicates correlation, not causation. An important feature does not necessarily drive outcomes. Additionally, if your model lacks features that have a causal impact on the target, what-if scenarios generated by the model may be invalid and highly misleading.

How can feature interactions cause misleading feature importance rankings?

---

Some models rank individual feature importance without accounting for interactions. Two features might seem unimportant individually but their interaction (product) could be the crucial explanatory variable. For example, linear regression would miss this interaction and rate both features as unimportant, while random forest might implicitly detect it but share the importance between the two features without revealing the interaction itself.

front: The Fowlkes-Mallows index is the [...] of [...] based on clustering and label assignments. Higher scores indicate better clustering performance.
back: The Fowlkes-Mallows index is the **geometric mean** of **precision and recall** based on clustering and label assignments. Higher scores indicate better clustering performance.

Why can't you just minimize inertia to find the best k-means clustering?

---

Inertia (sum of within-cluster variances) always decreases as you increase the number of clusters. Adding more clusters will always reduce inertia, but at some point the clusters stop being meaningful. This creates a tradeoff -- you need methods like the elbow method to find where adding more clusters stops providing substantial improvement.

What is the difference between internal and external clustering evaluation metrics?

---

**Internal metrics** assess cluster quality using only the input data (no labels needed). Examples: silhouette score, Davies-Bouldin index, inertia.

**External metrics** compare cluster assignments against ground truth labels when available. Examples: adjusted Rand index, normalized mutual information, Fowlkes-Mallows index.

front: k fold cross validation how it works
back:
How does k-fold cross-validation work, and what problem does it solve?

---

The training data is divided into k equal-sized folds. For each trial set of hyperparameters, the model trains on k-1 folds and tests on the remaining fold, rotating so every fold serves as validation once. Scores are aggregated across all folds. This solves the problem of overfitting to a single validation set and maximizes data utilization, since every data point is used for both training and validation.

front: K-fold cross-validation typically uses [...] folds to provide a robust estimate of model generalizability.
back: K-fold cross-validation typically uses **5 to 10** folds to provide a robust estimate of model generalizability.

Why is lasso regression particularly useful for feature selection?

---

Lasso's L1 penalty can shrink coefficients to exactly zero, meaning it effectively removes irrelevant features from the model. This makes it useful for feature selection and data compression, especially when dealing with sparse coefficients (where only a small number of variables significantly contribute to the dataset). Even in noisy (low SNR) environments, lasso remains a great feature selector.

front: Lasso regression uses an [...] penalty, which is the sum of [...] of coefficients.
back: Lasso regression uses an **L1** penalty, which is the sum of **absolute values** of coefficients.

Why does ordinary linear regression perform poorly in noisy (low signal-to-noise ratio) environments?

---

Ordinary linear regression has no penalty term to constrain its coefficients. In noisy environments, it tends to greatly overestimate coefficients, overshoot zero-valued coefficients, and assign large incorrect (even negative) coefficients. It is highly sensitive to outliers and noise. Regularized methods like ridge and lasso mitigate this by penalizing coefficient size.

front: MAE (Mean Absolute Error) is the average of the [...] between predicted and actual values. Compared to MSE, MAE is [...].
back: MAE (Mean Absolute Error) is the average of the **absolute differences** between predicted and actual values. Compared to MSE, MAE is **less sensitive to outliers**.

Before evaluating a regression model, it's useful to compare it against a "mean-value model." What is a mean-value model, and what does it tell you if your regression model can't beat it?

---

A mean-value model predicts the same value — the mean of the target variable in the training set — for every observation, regardless of input features. It completely ignores all predictors.

If your regression model doesn't beat this baseline (e.g. has a lower R² than ~0, or worse MAE/RMSE), it means your features are providing no useful information — the model has failed to learn any real relationship. It's the simplest possible sanity check: "Is using these features actually better than just guessing the average every time?"
